name: Changing Woman
description: Changing Woman, or Asdzáá Nádleehé in Navajo, is a central figure in Navajo mythology and one of the most important deities. She embodies the cyclical nature of life, renewal, and the passage of seasons. Born from the earth and sky, she ages from infancy to old age and then rejuvenates, constantly renewing herself and the earth along with her. She is the mother of the Hero Twins, Monster Slayer and Child-Born-of-Water, who rid the world of monsters, making it safe for humans. Changing Woman is associated with fertility, abundance, and the provision of sustenance. She is closely linked to the earth and its resources, and she taught the Navajo people how to cultivate corn and other crops. Her cyclical nature represents the importance of balance and harmony in the world. She resides in a Hogan made of turquoise in the Western Sea and is often associated with the colors turquoise, white shell, abalone, and jet. She is revered for her wisdom, kindness, and her ability to bring prosperity and wellbeing to the Navajo people. She represents the ideal of womanhood and motherhood in Navajo culture, embodying strength, resilience, and nurturing. Her stories emphasize the importance of living in harmony with nature and respecting the cyclical rhythms of life. She is considered a benevolent and powerful force, constantly working to ensure the wellbeing of her people and the health of the earth.
subtitle: The embodiment of cyclical renewal and life.

keyMyths.title: Birth of the Hero Twins
keyMyths.description: Changing Woman miraculously conceived and gave birth to the Hero Twins, Monster Slayer and Child-Born-of-Water, who were destined to rid the world of monsters.

keyMyths.title: Creation of the Navajo People
keyMyths.description: Changing Woman, using flakes of her own skin, created the first Navajo people, ensuring their survival and prosperity on the earth.

keyMyths.title: The Cyclical Nature
keyMyths.description: Changing Woman's ability to age and then rejuvenate embodies the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, reflecting the changing seasons and the renewal of the earth.

sources.author: Paul G. Zolbrod

sources.description: A retelling of the Navajo creation story.
sources.text: A retelling of the Navajo creation story.

worshipPractices.name: Prayer and Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Navajo people offer prayers and gifts to Changing Woman to seek her blessings for health, prosperity, and harmony in their lives. These offerings often include corn pollen, turquoise, and other precious materials.

worshipPractices.name: Sandpaintings
worshipPractices.description: Intricate sandpaintings depicting Changing Woman and scenes from her mythology are created during healing ceremonies. These sacred artworks serve as a temporary dwelling place for the deity, allowing her power to be channeled for healing purposes.

festivals.name: Kinaaldá
festivals.timing: Occurs after a girl's first menstruation
festivals.description: The Kinaaldá ceremony is a four-day rite of passage for young Navajo women, celebrating their transition into womanhood. It reenacts Changing Woman's creation and emphasizes her role as a symbol of fertility, strength, and renewal. The initiate is molded into the image of Changing Woman, embodying her virtues.

cultural.modernLegacy: Changing Woman remains a powerful symbol of Navajo identity, resilience, and the interconnectedness of all things. Her stories and teachings continue to inspire and guide Navajo people in their daily lives, reinforcing their cultural values and connection to the land. She is also increasingly recognized as an important figure in feminist spirituality and environmentalism.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Changing Woman's birth is a miraculous event, deeply intertwined with the creation of the Navajo world. She was born on Gobernador Knob (or, in some accounts, Hesperus Peak), emerging from the earth and sky, a union of the natural elements. Her birth is associated with the first light, symbolizing hope and new beginnings. As an infant, she aged rapidly, reaching adulthood within days. This accelerated growth signifies her connection to the cyclical nature of time and the constant renewal of the earth. Her unique origin story emphasizes her role as a bridge between the earthly and spiritual realms. She is not merely a deity but an embodiment of the land itself, its power, and its potential for regeneration. Her very existence is a testament to the Navajo belief in the inherent sacredness of the natural world.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Changing Woman's most significant story involves the creation of the Navajo people. Feeling lonely, she created people from flakes of her own skin. The first people she created were not able to sustain themselves, so she consulted with the Sun God, Tádídíín, who advised her to create people from the earth. Thus, she created the ancestors of the Navajo people. Another pivotal myth recounts her impregnation by the Sun, resulting in the birth of the Hero Twins, Monster Slayer and Child Born of Water. These twins embarked on a perilous journey to rid the world of monsters, ensuring the safety and prosperity of humankind. Changing Woman's guidance and support were crucial to their success. These stories highlight her role as a nurturer, protector, and creator, shaping the destiny of the Navajo people and ensuring their survival in a challenging world. She is a symbol of strength, resilience, and the enduring power of creation.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Worship of Changing Woman is deeply embedded in Navajo culture and spiritual practices. She is revered as a benevolent and powerful deity who embodies the qualities of beauty, fertility, and harmony. Prayers and offerings are made to her to seek her blessings for health, prosperity, and well-being. The Kinaaldá ceremony, a four-day rite of passage for young women, is a central expression of devotion to Changing Woman. During this ceremony, the initiate is symbolically transformed into Changing Woman, embodying her virtues and preparing for her role as a woman in Navajo society. Sandpaintings depicting Changing Woman are created during healing ceremonies, serving as a temporary dwelling place for the deity and allowing her power to be channeled for healing purposes. These practices demonstrate the deep reverence and connection that Navajo people have with Changing Woman, recognizing her as a vital source of strength, guidance, and renewal.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Changing Woman's legacy extends far beyond the realm of mythology, deeply influencing Navajo culture, art, and spirituality. She is a potent symbol of female empowerment, representing strength, resilience, and the ability to adapt and overcome challenges. Her association with the cyclical nature of life and the seasons resonates with contemporary environmental concerns, inspiring a renewed appreciation for the interconnectedness of all things and the importance of sustainable living. In modern Navajo art, Changing Woman is often depicted in vibrant colors, embodying beauty, harmony, and the life-giving power of the earth. Her stories and teachings continue to be passed down through generations, reinforcing Navajo cultural values and providing guidance for navigating the complexities of modern life. Changing Woman remains a timeless and enduring figure, inspiring hope, resilience, and a deep connection to the land and its sacred traditions.

shortDescription: A central figure in Navajo mythology embodying the cyclical nature of life, renewal, and the seasons. Mother of the Hero Twins.
